event: Prehistoric stone tools and rock fragments indicate that hunter-gatherer communities inhabited the region during the Upper Paleolithic and later periods.
Neolithic communities settle in the savanna and forest zones, developing polished stone tools and early agriculture.
Iron-working spreads through the region as farming and settled village life expand across the area.
The first organized societies and trade settlements form, linked to the trans-Saharan trade networks of the western Sudan.
Senufo and other agricultural peoples establish settled communities and craft traditions in the north.
The northern region falls within the sphere of the Mali Empire, channeling gold and kola nut trade northward.
Mande-speaking Dyula traders expand commercial networks, founding the trading town of Kong as a Muslim merchant center.
Portuguese navigators reach the coast, naming the area for its abundant ivory trade and establishing early European contact.
French missionaries land near Assinie on the eastern coast, marking one of the earliest French presences in the region.
The Kingdom of Kong rises as a powerful Dyula-led commercial and Islamic state controlling regional trade routes.
Akan-speaking groups, including the Baoulé and Agni, migrate into the region from the Ashanti area of present-day Ghana.
Queen Abla Pokou leads the Baoulé people in a legendary migration, founding the Baoulé kingdom in the central region.
France signs treaties with coastal chiefs at Grand-Bassam and Assinie, establishing protectorates and trading posts.
France formally declares Côte d'Ivoire a French colony, separating it administratively from Senegal.
The colony is incorporated into the federation of French West Africa (Afrique Occidentale Française).
French forces capture Samori Touré, ending his Wassoulou Empire's resistance and securing colonial control of the interior.
Governor Gabriel Angoulvant launches a violent 'pacification' campaign to subdue persistent African resistance in the interior.
French military campaigns largely suppress the long-running Baoulé and other anti-colonial uprisings.
Abidjan becomes the capital of the colony, growing into a major commercial and administrative hub.
Félix Houphouët-Boigny founds the African Agricultural Union to defend African planters against forced labor practices.
Forced labor is abolished and Houphouët-Boigny co-founds the Rassemblement Démocratique Africain, becoming a key nationalist leader.
Côte d'Ivoire becomes an autonomous republic within the French Community following a referendum.
Côte d'Ivoire gains full independence from France on 7 August, with Félix Houphouët-Boigny as its first president.
Côte d'Ivoire is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Côte d'Ivoire becomes a founding member of the Organisation of African Unity.
The country experiences strong economic growth fueled by cocoa and coffee exports, an era dubbed the 'Ivorian Miracle.'
Yamoussoukro, Houphouët-Boigny's birthplace, is designated the official capital of the country.
Multiparty politics is reintroduced, and Houphouët-Boigny wins the first contested presidential election against Laurent Gbagbo.
Construction of the Basilica of Our Lady of Peace in Yamoussoukro is completed, becoming one of the world's largest churches.
President Félix Houphouët-Boigny dies after 33 years in power, succeeded by Henri Konan Bédié.
General Robert Guéï seizes power in the country's first military coup, ousting President Bédié.
Laurent Gbagbo becomes president after a disputed election and popular uprising forces Guéï from power.
A failed coup attempt sparks a civil war, splitting the country between the rebel-held north and government-controlled south.
The Linas-Marcoussis peace accord is signed in France, creating a power-sharing government to end the conflict.
Renewed fighting erupts, and French peacekeepers destroy the Ivorian air force after a government airstrike kills French soldiers.
The Ouagadougou Political Agreement is signed, making rebel leader Guillaume Soro prime minister and easing tensions.
A disputed presidential election between Laurent Gbagbo and Alassane Ouattara triggers a violent post-election crisis.
Gbagbo is captured with French and UN backing, Ouattara takes office, and the crisis leaves around 3,000 people dead.
Alassane Ouattara is reelected president in a relatively peaceful election amid economic recovery.
A jihadist attack at the beach resort of Grand-Bassam kills 19 people, marking the country's first major terrorist assault.
Voters approve a new constitution establishing a Third Republic with a vice presidency and senate.
The International Criminal Court acquits Laurent Gbagbo of crimes against humanity related to the post-election violence.
Alassane Ouattara wins a contested third term amid opposition boycotts and deadly unrest over its legality.
Côte d'Ivoire remains one of West Africa's largest economies and the world's leading cocoa producer.
